volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Comedy of Asses (Section 2.4.475 - Section 3.2.560)

sections.label: Section 2.4.475
sections.original: ni istum impudicum percies .
sections.translation: if you don ’ t proclaim this shameless fellow .

sections.label: Section 2.4.475b
sections.original: Perii hercle . age impudice ,
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ m undone . Be off , you shameless fellow .

sections.label: Section 2.4.476
sections.original: sceleste , non audes mihi scelesto subvenire ?
sections.translation: You rascal .

sections.label: Section 2.4.477
sections.original: Pergin precari pessimo ?
sections.translation: Do you persist in soliciting the scamp ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.477b
sections.original: Quae res ? tun libero homini
sections.translation: How ’ s this ? ( To LEONIDA .) Do you , rascal , who are a slave , speak abusively to a free man ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.478b
sections.original: Vapula .
sections.translation: Give him a beating .

sections.label: Section 2.4.478c
sections.original: Id quidém tibi hercle fiet ,
sections.translation: By my faith , that surely shall befall yourself to get a beating as soon as ever I shall see Demaenetus this day .

sections.label: Section 2.4.480
sections.original: in ius voco te .
sections.translation: I summon you to judgments [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.480b
sections.original: Non eo .
sections.translation: I shan ’ t go .

sections.label: Section 2.4.480c
sections.original: Non is ? memento .
sections.translation: You won ’ t go ? Remember —

sections.label: Section 2.4.480d
sections.original: Memini .
sections.translation: I do remember .

sections.label: Section 2.4.481
sections.original: Dabitur pol supplicium mihi de tergo vostro .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ ll have satisfaction out of your back .

sections.label: Section 2.4.481b
sections.original: Vae te .
sections.translation: Woe unto you ? What , villain — satisfaction to be given by us to you indeed ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.482b
sections.original: Atque etiam
sections.translation: Aye , and even this very day satisfaction shall be given me for your abusive language .

sections.label: Section 2.4.484_485
sections.original: Quid , verbero ? ain tu , furcifer ? erum nos fugitare censes ?
sections.translation: How now , whip-knave ? How say you , hang-dog ? Do you suppose that we shall run away from our master ? Go this instant then to our master , where you were citing us just now , and where you were wishing to go .

sections.label: Section 2.4.487
sections.original: Nunc demum ? tamen numquam hinc feres argenti nummum , nisi me
sections.translation: What , now at last ? Still , you shall never get a coin of money away from me , unless Demaenetus shall order me to give it .

sections.label: Section 2.4.488b
sections.original: Ita facito , age ambula ergo .
sections.translation: Do so . Come , move on then . Are you to offer insults to another person , and are they not to be repeated to yourself ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.490
sections.original: tam ego homo sum quam tu .
sections.translation: I ’ m a man as much as you are .

sections.label: Section 2.4.490b
sections.original: Scilicet . ita res est .
sections.translation: No doubt such is the fact .

sections.label: Section 2.4.490c
sections.original: Sequere hac ergo .
sections.translation: Follow me this way , then . With your good leave [*] I would now say this : not a person has ever accused me by reason of my deserving it , nor is there in Athens one other individual , this day , whom they would think they could as safely trust .

sections.label: Section 2.4.493b
sections.original: Fortassis . sed tamen me
sections.translation: Perhaps so : but still , you shall never this day persuade me to entrust to you , whom I don ’ t know , this money .

sections.label: Section 2.4.495
sections.original: lupus est homo homini , non homo , quom qualis sit non novit .
sections.translation: A man to a man is a wolf [*] , not a man , when the other doesn ’ t know of what character he is .

sections.label: Section 2.4.496
sections.original: Iam nunc secunda mihi facis . scibam huic te capitulo hodie
sections.translation: Now at last you are appeasing me [*] : I was sure that this day you would give satisfaction to this poor head of mine ; although I ’ m in mean garb , still , I ’ m well to do , nor can an estimate of my means be formed from it .

sections.label: Section 2.4.499
sections.original: Fortasse .
sections.translation: Perhaps so .

sections.label: Section 2.4.499b
sections.original: Etiam † nunc dico Periphanes Rhodo mercator dives
sections.translation: Still more then I tell you : Periphanes , a merchant of Rhodes , a rich man ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.500
sections.original: absente ero solus mihi talentum argenti soli
sections.translation: in the absence of my master , himself alone paid over to me , in private , a talent of silver , and trusted me , nor was he deceived in it .

sections.label: Section 2.4.502
sections.original: Fortasse .
sections.translation: Perhaps so .

sections.label: Section 2.4.502b
sections.original: Atque etiam tu quoque ipse , si esses percontatus
sections.translation: And you , too , yourself , as well , if you had enquired about me of other people , would , i ’ faith , I ’ m quite sure , have entrusted to me what you now have with you .

sections.label: Section 2.4.503b
sections.original: Haud negassim .—
sections.translation: I don ’ t deny it .

sections.label: Section 3.1.504
sections.original: Néqueon ego ted interdictis facere mansuetem meis ?
sections.translation: And am I unable to render you obedient to my injunctions ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.505
sections.original: an ita tu es animata , ut qui matris éxpers imperio sies ?
sections.translation: Or are you so disposed as to be free from the control of your mother ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.506
sections.original: Vbi piem Pietatem , si istoc more moratam tibi
sections.translation: How could I propitiate Piety [*] , if I could desire to please you , being endowed with these manners , after the fashion , mother , that you enjoin upon me ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.508
sections.original: An decorum est adversari meis te praeceptis ?
sections.translation: Is it consistent with propriety for you to oppose my precepts ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.508b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.509
sections.original: Hocine est pietatem colere , matris impérium minuere ?
sections.translation: Is this worshipping Piety , to lessen the authority of a mother ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.510
sections.original: Neque quae recte faciunt culpo neque quae delinquont amo .
sections.translation: Those who act right I blame not , nor do I love those who do wrong .

sections.label: Section 3.1.511
sections.original: Satis dicacula es amatrix .
sections.translation: You are a very prating , lovesick girl .

sections.label: Section 3.1.511b
sections.original: Mater , is quaestus mihi est :
sections.translation: Mother , that is my living [*] . His tongue woos me , his person seeks me , his passion pleads , opportunity prompts .

sections.label: Section 3.1.513
sections.original: Ego te volui castigare , tu mi accusatrix ades .
sections.translation: I was purposing to convince you . Are you come as my accuser ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.514
sections.original: Neque edepol te accuso neque id me facere fas existimo .
sections.translation: By my troth , I neither do accuse you , nor do I think it right I should do so ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.515
sections.original: verum ego meas queror fortunas , cum illo quém amo prohibeor .
sections.translation: but I do complain of my lot , when I am separated from him whom I love .

sections.label: Section 3.1.516
sections.original: Ecqua pars orationis de die dabitur mihi ?
sections.translation: Will then one bit of the whole day ’ s talk be left for myself ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.517
sections.original: Et meam partem loquendi ét tuam trado tibi ;
sections.translation: Both my share of the speaking and your own do I give up to you . Do you yourself keep the signal [*] both for speaking and for being silent .

sections.label: Section 3.1.520
sections.original: ubi quiesco , omnis familiae causa consistit tibi .
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , if I only put up my oars in the boat-house [*] while I ’ m resting , all the welfare of the household is at a standstill for you .

sections.label: Section 3.1.521
sections.original: Quid ais tu , quam ego unam vidi mulierem audacissimam ?
sections.translation: How say you , the out and out most insolent woman that ever I saw ? How often have I forbidden you to speak to Argyrippus , the son of Demaenetus , or to touch him , or to hold discourse with him , or to look at him ? What has he ever given ? What has he ordered to be brought to our house ? Or do you fancy to yourself

sections.label: Section 3.1.525
sections.original: verba blanda esse aurum rere , dicta docta pro datis ?
sections.translation: that smooth words are gold ?— that clever speeches are as good as presents ? Of your own accord you fell in love with him ; of your own accord you go after him ; of your own accord you request him to be sent for to you . Those who are givers , those same you laugh at ; those who are cheating us , you are dying for . If any one promises you that he ’ ll make you rich when his mother dies , ought you to be waiting for that ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.530
sections.original: † ecastor nobis periclum magnum et familiae portenditur ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , a great risk impends over ourselves and the household , that we may die of hunger while we are awaiting her death . Now therefore , unless he brings me here twenty minae of silver , upon my word , though profuse of his tears , he shall certainly be turned from here out of doors . This day ’ s the end of [*] excuses for poverty at my house .

sections.label: Section 3.1.535
sections.original: Patiar , si cibo carere me iubes , mater mea .
sections.translation: If , my mother , you were to order me to go without victuals , I would submit .

sections.label: Section 3.1.536
sections.original: Non voto téd amare qui dant quoia amentur gratia .
sections.translation: I don ’ t forbid you to love those who give that for the sake of which they ought to be loved .

sections.label: Section 3.1.537
sections.original: Quid si hic animus occupatust , mater , quid faciam ? mone .
sections.translation: What , mother , if this inclination of mine is fixed ? What am I to do ? Tell me .

sections.label: Section 3.1.537b
sections.original: Em ,
sections.translation: Oh dear — look at my head [*] , if , indeed , you consider your own interest .

sections.label: Section 3.1.539_540
sections.original: Etiam ópilio qui pascit , mater , alienas ovis ,
sections.translation: Even the shepherd , mother , that feeds the sheep of another , has a certain one of his own to be the consoler of his hopes . For the sake of my affection , do allow me to love Argyrippus only , who is my choice .

sections.label: Section 3.1.543
sections.original: Intro abi , nam te quidem edepol nihil est impudentius .
sections.translation: Go in-doors , for , upon my word , there is really nothing more impudent than yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.1.544
sections.original: Audientem dicto , mater , produxisti filiam .
sections.translation: Mother , you have given birth to a daughter obedient to your commands .

sections.label: Section 3.2.545
sections.original: Perfídiae laudes grátiasque habémus merito mágnas ,
sections.translation: Great praise and thanks we give deservedly to perfidy , when relying upon our tricks , our stratagems , and our devices , upon our confidence in our shoulder-blades and the hardihood resulting from the elm-twigs so oft applied , against the whips , the searing-irons , the crosses , and the fetters ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.549_550
sections.original: nervos , catenas , carceres , numellas , pedicas , boias
sections.translation: the cords , the chains , the prisons , the stocks , the shackles , the collars , and taskmasters most cruel and well acquainted with our backs , who many a time before have imprinted [*] scars upon our shoulder-blades ; by conquering , now , these legions , troops and armies of thieves ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.555
sections.original: vi pugnando periuriis nostris fugae potiti .
sections.translation: by our prowess , through our perjuries , O brave , have we gained the victory . This , through the valour of this comrade of mine , and through my own courtesy , has been brought about .

sections.label: Section 3.2.557
sections.original: factumst . qui me vir fortior ad sufferundas plagas ?
sections.translation: What man is there more firm than myself at enduring stripes ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.558
sections.original: Edepol virtutes qui tuas non possis conlaudare
sections.translation: By the powers , you who can extol your exploits now , as I can do exploits , which in peace and in warfare you have so — villanously performed ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.560
sections.original: ne illa edepol pro merito tuo memorari multa possunt :
sections.translation: verily , in troth , many in number may they be now recounted according to your deserts ; where you have defrauded him that trusted you , where you have proved faithless to your master , where knowingly and wilfully you have on solemn oath been perjured , where you have bored through party walls , where you have been detected in theft , where you have full oft pleaded your cause , as you hung up , against eight
